turns.content: what's the difference between using the guard macro for async validation versus writing a custom validator trait manually?

The main distinction lies in how you handle asynchronous operations and define rules. The garde crate provides an async-aware derive macro that lets you attach validators to structs directly, whereas manual traits require you to implement the logic yourself and potentially use a separate validation context.

I thought I could just call the guard method on any struct without modifying it first.

I'm trying to build a proof-of-concept validator for my hobby project using the garde crate, but I keep getting errors about missing traits. I thought deriving Serialize on my struct would be enough since serde_json handles that.

That is a common misconception. The garde crate does not rely on serde's Serialize trait for its core validation logic, so you do not need to implement or derive it unless you are specifically using the async adapter which requires custom types that might need serialization later. For standard synchronous validation with the rules DSL, just define your struct normally.

Okay, I defined a User struct and created some simple rules like email format checks using the built-in validators. But when I try to run the async version of the validator with the new adapter, it fails because my custom type is not recognized.

The issue lies in how you are defining your custom types for the async adapter. When using the garde crate's async capabilities, any struct you want to validate must implement the specific trait required by that adapter, which is different from standard Serialize implementations.

I tried implementing that trait manually but I am getting a compilation error saying the field types are incompatible with what the rule system expects. Is there a way to map my custom fields directly without complex conversions?

You do not need complex mappings if you structure your data correctly from the start. The best approach is to define your validation rules DSL to match exactly the shape of your struct, ensuring that every field in your type corresponds directly to a rule or an optional validator in your configuration.

I managed to get the basic async flow working now. However, I am wondering if there is a way to extend the rules DSL with my own custom validators that do something very specific like checking for historical data consistency without relying on external crates.

Yes, you can easily create your own validators by implementing the trait provided by the crate. This allows you to write custom logic inline within your validation pipeline, giving you full control over complex edge cases like your historical data requirement without needing to pull in additional dependencies.

That sounds perfect for my use case. One last thing though, I noticed the documentation mentions something about combining synchronous and asynchronous flows. Can we actually chain both of them together seamlessly?

You can absolutely combine them by using the appropriate adapters for each context. If you need to run a quick check synchronously before an expensive async validation, you simply call the synchronous validator first and then pass its result into the async adapter's input type.

It's like when I tried to debug a failing CI/CD pipeline earlier and realized my validation logic was just throwing errors instead of returning structured data. I'm building a new form validator in Rust and thought maybe the garde crate could help me avoid those messy error strings.

That's a great comparison because handling unstructured error messages is exactly why you want to use a proper validation crate like garde. The core idea with garde is that it gives you a rules DSL where you define constraints on fields, and then the system automatically generates the right error types for your application instead of manual string concatenation.

I've been using serde_json::Value to hold form data but I'm struggling with how to actually apply those rules. Is there a derive macro that automatically wires up the validation logic so I don't have to manually write loops checking every field?

Yes, you can use the derive macros on your structs along with guards to define constraints like length limits or regex patterns. For example, if you create a struct that derives From<serde_json::Value>, garde will automatically validate it against those rules and return a Result type instead of panicking.
I tried writing a custom validator function but I keep getting compiler errors about lifetime elision. Does garde support async validation or do I have to stick with synchronous checks?

You can definitely use async validation by combining garde with tokio and the futures library, though you need to be careful about how you structure your async blocks. The main thing is that garde itself provides synchronous rules DSL but it integrates well with async runtimes when you wrap its output in an Async trait implementation.
I think I understand the basic flow now, but what happens if my custom validator throws a panic instead of returning an error? Will the CI/CD pipeline just fail silently?

That's actually exactly the common mistake beginners make when working with validation crates. If your custom validator panics, it will crash the entire runtime and you'll get no structured error logs to debug what went wrong.
So how do I prevent that panic from happening? Should I wrap my logic in a try block or use something else?

You should avoid using panic entirely and instead return an error using the Result type. When you write your custom validator, make sure it returns Ok(some_value) on success or Err(error_message) on failure so garde can handle the error gracefully.
I'm still confused about how to integrate this into my existing CI/CD pipeline. Does garde work with cargo test or do I need a separate validation step?

You can integrate garde directly into your cargo test suite by running the validation checks as part of your test harness. The crate will automatically catch invalid inputs during your tests and generate detailed error reports that you can use to improve your pipeline.
One more thing I'm wondering about is how does garde handle nested structures? Like if I have a user object with an address field, will it validate the address recursively?

Yes, garde handles recursive validation automatically when you define rules on nested structs. If your User struct contains an Address sub-struct and you apply a rule to Address, the entire validation chain will check both levels without extra code.
Wait, so I don't have to manually unwrap values or create intermediate variables? That sounds like it would save me tons of boilerplate code compared to what I'm doing now.
Exactly. The derive macros and rules DSL handle all the recursive unwrapping for you, which means your code stays clean and focused on business logic rather than validation plumbing.

Hey, I'm new to this open-source project and I want to add some input validation. I've heard about a crate called garde that uses rules DSL for async validation. How do I actually set up the derive macro on my struct?

Great choice for a startup! You just need to add the derive attribute from garde::prelude or garde itself to your struct definition. For example, you would write #[derive(garde::Validate)] right above your struct. Then in your async function body, you can call the validate method on that instance.
Wait, hold on. If I add #[derive(garde::Validate)] and then run my code, will it automatically generate all the validation logic for me? Or do I still need to write custom rules manually?

You're right to question that. The derive macro generates default checks like field existence and basic type constraints, but you definitely cannot skip writing custom rules if your validation logic is complex. You'll need to add manual rules using the RulesDSL builder or pass a garde::Rules instance directly into the Validate trait implementation.
So that means I can't just rely on the macro for everything? Like, if I have nested structs with custom constraints, I'll still need to build those rules myself?

Exactly. The derive macro is great for boilerplate, but garde shines when you need complex business logic beyond basic field checks. For example, if your form requires that a total price must not exceed the budget limit, or that two fields match each other, you'll have to construct those rules manually using the RulesDSL API.
That sounds tedious for a small startup. Is there any way to make this less verbose? Can I use macros or helpers to avoid writing all that boilerplate?

You can definitely reduce verbosity by using the helper methods in garde's builder pattern. Instead of manually chaining every rule, you can compose rules into a single object and then attach it to your struct via the Validate trait. This keeps the code readable without needing hundreds of lines of manual rule definitions.
Okay, so I still need to manually define my custom validators but just not write raw async loops? Can you give me a concrete example where I'm building rules for a nested struct?

Sure. Imagine you have an Order struct with a nested ShippingAddress. You can build your rules using the RulesDSL builder to add constraints like length checks or format validators, then attach those rules to your struct via #[derive(garde::Validate)] and pass the compiled Rules instance into your async validation flow.
I see. So the derive macro handles the async execution part but I still need to manually construct the Rules DSL object? That seems like a lot of setup for something that should be automatic.

You're right, it feels like more work than just adding an attribute. But garde is designed so you only pay this cost when your validation logic actually grows complex enough to matter. If you only need basic checks, the derive macro alone will do the job perfectly fine.
Okay, fair point. But what if I want to extend garde with my own custom validators? Do I have to re-implement the whole trait or just add a simple method?

You don't need to reimplement anything. Just implement your custom validator as a function that takes serde::de::DeserializeOwned and returns Option<garde::error::Validation>, then register it with garde's builder so it gets picked up automatically when you compile the rules.
That sounds much cleaner. So I can keep using the derive macro for basic stuff and only write custom functions when I need something special?

Precisely. That hybrid approach is exactly how most teams use garde in production codebases. You get the safety of the macro-generated boilerplate while still having full control over complex rules without reinventing the validation engine itself.
One last question: if I'm running this in a startup environment where async is everywhere, does garde handle that automatically or do I need to wrap it?

garde already supports async validation out of the box through its trait definitions. You just call validate() on your struct in an async context and it returns a future that resolves once all rules have been checked, so you don't need extra wrappers.

I'm trying to port my validation logic from Python's Pydantic into Rust, but I keep feeling like I'm reinventing the wheel. In Python we just chain attributes together with dot notation for rules, but in Rust it feels messy without a good DSL or a custom validator pattern.

You are describing exactly why the garde crate exists; it provides that missing DSL layer so you don't have to write raw predicates. Think of garde as a structured way to define rules like 'field must match email regex' or 'length between 6 and 12' using a declarative syntax instead of imperative code, which is similar to how Pydantic defines schemas but with Rust's type safety.

I see the benefit, but I'm still confused about how to actually write these rules. Do I have to manually create a validator struct for every field like I did in Python? Is there a way to just derive something?

You do not need manual structs because garde supports macro expansion or derivation traits that automatically generate validators from your data structures. The crate lets you define validation rules inline on types, so instead of writing a separate validator function for each field, you annotate fields with attributes like 'required' or 'regex' and the compiler generates the logic for you.
That sounds convenient, but what if I need something more complex? Like validating that two fields in my struct match each other, which isn't just a simple attribute?

For complex cross-field rules like matching emails or ensuring consistency between fields, you can use custom validators implemented as traits. The garde crate allows you to implement a trait that takes two field values and returns a validation result, so you can express relationships like 'these two must match' without losing type safety.
I'm still worried about performance since I'm porting from Python where validation is very fast. Does garde add overhead?

The runtime cost of using the derive macros or custom traits in garde is minimal because the generated code is optimized Rust, and it avoids the dynamic dispatch overhead that often plagues Python validation libraries. The main performance concern comes from async patterns if you need to perform expensive checks.
Ah, I see. So what about async validation? Can I use garde with async runtimes?

Yes, the crate supports async validation through specific traits that allow you to define validators that can be awaited in an async context. This means you can write validators that perform network calls or database queries while maintaining the same DSL style.
Perfect, so I should just import garde and use its derive macros to get started with my Rust port?

Exactly. You start by adding the crate to your dependencies, then apply the appropriate attributes or derive macros to your structs to enable validation rules without writing boilerplate code manually.
I'm getting this error when I try to use the guard validator on my User struct: 'Cannot coerce into Into<guard::Value>'. What went wrong?

The issue is that you're likely trying to pass a plain Rust struct directly where an async validation context is expected. The garde crate requires the value being validated to implement specific traits so it can be coerced into guard::Value for the async runtime. You need to ensure your User type derives the necessary trait implementations or wraps it properly before passing it to the validator function.
I already added derive(Guard) to my struct and it's in the Cargo.toml dependencies, so I thought that was enough. But maybe the problem is with how I'm calling the validation function?

It sounds like you might be confusing the guard::Validate trait implementation requirement with just deriving Guard. The derive macro generates the necessary implementations for specific traits, but if your code isn't actually invoking the async validation path correctly, that coercion will still fail. You need to ensure you're calling the guard function in a context where it can properly handle the async nature of the validation.
I'm not sure what you mean by 'async validation path'. I thought garde was just synchronous like regular serde validators. Can't I just call .validate() directly on my struct?

While garde does support synchronous patterns via the derive macro, the modern approach often involves async validation for better performance and composability with other async frameworks. When you use the guard::Validate trait or call functions expecting guard::Value, they expect an async runtime context to be available. If you're trying to run it in a purely sync context without proper setup, that's where the coercion error comes from.
So I should just wrap my struct in something? Like maybe a Box or Arc?

You don't necessarily need to wrap it in a Box or Arc unless you're sharing ownership. The real fix is ensuring your code actually calls the async guard function properly, not just relying on the derive macro alone. You might want to check if you're using the correct import paths and making sure your validation logic respects the async boundaries that garde enforces.
I see now. I was trying to call it in a sync function but garde requires an async context. Can you show me how to properly set up an async guard validator?

To set this up correctly, you need to ensure your validation logic runs within an async block or uses the appropriate trait methods from garde that support async execution. The guard crate provides specific traits and functions designed for this scenario so you can properly coerce your types into the expected guard::Value format without manual wrapping.
That makes sense, but I'm still confused about how the derive macro interacts with the async requirement. Does it automatically make everything async?

The derive macro generates implementations for specific traits that enable validation, but it doesn't magically turn your entire function into an async one. You still need to ensure you're calling the guard functions in a way that respects their async nature, which might involve using tokio or another runtime context.
Okay so I need to call it inside an async function? And maybe use the guard::Validate trait explicitly?

Yes, you should wrap your validation logic in an async context and ensure you're using the guard functions that expect async input. The derive macro helps by providing the necessary trait implementations, but you still need to call the right functions in the right context.
Got it. One more question - can I mix sync and async validation if I only need some fields checked synchronously?

You can partially validate synchronously by implementing specific traits for individual fields, but garde's modern approach encourages full async consistency to avoid context switching issues. Mixing them might work technically but could lead to performance problems or unexpected behavior in larger systems.
